content.problemStatement:
Chef has 333 boxes of sizes AAA, BBB, and CCC respectively. He puts the boxes in bags of size DDD (A≤B≤C≤DA \leq B \leq C \leq DA≤B≤C≤D). Find the minimum number of bags Chef needs so that he can put each box in a bag. A bag can contain more than one box if the sum of sizes of boxes in the bag does not exceed the size of the bag.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT denoting the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains four integers AAA, BBB, CCC, and DDD on a single line denoting the sizes of the boxes and bags.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line the minimum number of bags Chef needs.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated integers A, B, C and D (1 ≤ A ≤ B ≤ C ≤ D ≤ 100) representing the sizes of the three boxes and the size of a bag.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of bags required to store all three boxes.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: 2+3+5=10 equals the bag size, so one bag suffices.\nTest case 2: 1+2+3=6>5, but 1+2=3≤5, so boxes 1 and 2 share a bag and box 3 uses another → 2 bags.\nTest case 3: No two boxes fit together (3+3>4, 3+4>4, 3+4>4), therefore each needs its own bag → 3 bags.
First case: total size 3>2, but 1+1=2 fits, so 2 bags. Second case: 4+5+6=15 equals bag size, so 1 bag.
All boxes have size 5 and the bag also size 5, so each box needs its own bag.

solution.approach:
Because the boxes are already given in non‑decreasing order (A ≤ B ≤ C) we only need to consider three possibilities:
1. If A+B+C ≤ D, all three boxes fit in one bag → answer = 1.
2. Otherwise, the smallest possible pair of boxes is A+B. If A+B ≤ D we can put A and B together and C alone → answer = 2.
3. If even A+B > D, no pair can fit (any other pair is larger) → each box needs its own bag → answer = 3.
The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case.
